                 Employment and Earnings is prepared by the
             Division of Industry Employment Statistics and the
             Division of Employment and Unemployment Anal-
             ysis in collaboration with the Division of Special
             Publications. The data are collected by the Bureau
             of the Census (Department of Commerce), State
             Employment Security Agencies, and State Depart-
             ments of Labor in cooperation with the Bureau of
             Labor Statistics. A brief description of the co-
             operative statistical programs of the BLS with
             these agencies is presented in the Explanatory
             Notes. The State agencies are listed on the inside
             back cover.

             through the Superintendent of Documents, U.S.

                         Vol. 25 No. 2 February 1978
                         Editors: Chester L. Green, Gloria P. Green, Marcy Freedman




                                    Editors' Note

                                Household survey data for periods prior to January 1978 are not strictly comparable with
                                current data because of the introduction of an expansion in the sample and revisions in the
                                estimation procedures. As a result, the overall civilian labor force and employment totals in
                                January were raised by roughly a quarter of a million; unemployment levels and rates were
                                essentially unchanged. An explanation of the procedural changes and an indication of the
                                differences appear in "Revisions in the Current Population Survey in January 1978," in
                                this issue of Employment and Earnings.

                                                                      Explanatory Notes

                                  These explanatory notes provide information on the concepts, methodology, and scope of Household Data
                                  (A tables), Establishment Data (B, C, and D tables), and State and Area Unemployment Data (E table) pub-
                                  lished ir Employment and Earnings.

                    Labor turnover is the gross movement of wage and salary work-       stoppage extends through the report period.
                ers into and out of employed status with respect to individual
                establishments. This movement, which relates to a calendar month,
                is divided into two broad types: Accession (new hires and rehires)      ESTIMATING METHODS
                and separations (terminations of employment initiated by either
                employer or employee). Each type of action is cumulated for a               The principal features of the procedure used to estimate em-
                calendar month and expressed as a rate per 100 employees. The           ployment for the industry statistics are (1) the use of the "link
                date relate to all employees, whether full- or part-time, permanent     relative" technique, which is a form of ratio estimation, (2) periodic
                or temporary, including executive, office, sales, other salaried        adjustment of employment levels to new benchmarks, and (3) the
                personnel, and production workers. Transfers to another establish-      use of size and regional stratification.
                ment of the company are included, beginning with January 1959.
                    Accessions are the total number of permanent and temporary
                additions to the employment roll, including both new and rehired
                employees.                                                              The "link relative" technique

                ployment roll of persons who have never before been employed in         ployment to that of the previous month is computed. This is
                the establishment (except employees transferring from another           called a link relative. The estimates of employment (all employees,
                establishment of the same company) or of former employees               including production and nonproduction workers together) for
                not recalled by the employer.                                           the current month are obtained by multiplying the estimates for

                                                                      Seasonal adjustment

                   Many economic statistics reflect a regularly recurring seasonal       adjusted series-it is important to note that seasonal adjustment
                movement which can be estimated on the basis of past experience.         is merely an approximation based on past experience. Seasonally
                By eliminating that part of the change which can be ascribed to          adjusted estimates have a broader margin of possible error than the
                usual seasonal variation, it is possible to observe the cyclical and     original data on which they are based, since they are subject not
                other nonseasonal movements in the series. However, in evaluating        only to sampling and other errors, but in addition, are affected by
                deviations from the seasonal pattern—that is, changes in a seasonally    the uncertainties of the seasonal adjustment process itself. Sea-
